usage: Papers/Research archive page hero — raven dissolving into citation graphs, paper cross-references, and knowledge taxonomy trees
concept: Raven perched on stacked volumes, its plumage dissolving into citation network graphs, DOI cross-reference links, taxonomy trees, and structured data tables from published research
prompt: A highly detailed 18th century natural history engraving of a common raven (Corvus corax) perched atop a stack of three leather-bound folio volumes, rendered in fine black ink cross-hatching on white background. The raven faces three-quarters right, head tilted downward as if studying. On the left half, the raven is rendered in full classical engraving style with precise feather detail: the glossy black plumage suggested through dense cross-hatching, the thick curved bill drawn with anatomical precision, the intelligent piercing eye as focal point. Starting from the right wing and tail, the engraving gradually dissolves and transforms: the layered wing feathers become citation network graph edges connecting circular paper nodes, each node containing tiny parallel dashes suggesting DOI identifiers. The tail feathers transform into branching taxonomy tree structures with hierarchical classification levels fanning downward. The breast feathers on the right side morph into rows of a structured data table with thin ruled lines and small rectangular cells. The throat hackles dissolve into interconnected flowchart diamond decision nodes representing peer review stages. The books beneath remain fully rendered in classical engraving style, grounding the composition, but the pages of the topmost open volume show tiny graph structures instead of text — miniature scatter plots and bar charts rendered in the same cross-hatching technique. The dissolution is gradual and seamless — the same line weight, the same hand-drawn precision throughout. Where raven plumage ends and research infrastructure begins is beautifully ambiguous. The technical elements maintain the organic flowing quality of natural history illustration. Black ink on white paper throughout, no color, no sepia, no tinting. One unified composition where natural intelligence and structured knowledge are drawn as the same substance. Extremely fine line detail, museum quality engraving meets scientific publication elegance.
